title: URL Slug Generator (SEO-friendly URLs)
shortDescription: Convert any title or sentence into an SEO-friendly URL slug. Configurable separator, length cap, lowercase, and Unicode support.
description: Type or paste a title; the generator strips punctuation, normalizes accents, collapses whitespace, and produces a clean slug. Supports hyphens, underscores, or dots; configurable max length; optional Unicode preservation for non-Latin URLs.
metaDescription: Free URL slug generator. SEO-friendly slugs from any title. Hyphen/underscore/dot separators, max length, Unicode support, ASCII-only mode.

sections.heading: What's a URL slug
paragraphs: A slug is the human-readable part of a URL after the domain — for example 'how-to-bake-bread' in '/blog/how-to-bake-bread'. Good slugs are short, descriptive, and stable. Search engines and humans both read them as part of the page's identity.
Standard rules: lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens only. Spaces become hyphens. Punctuation is removed. Stop words (the, a, is) may be omitted for brevity, though it's a stylistic choice.

sections.heading: ASCII vs Unicode slugs
paragraphs: ASCII slugs (the default): Latin letters and digits only. Universally supported, never URL-encoded, share-friendly. The standard choice for SEO-focused English/multilingual blogs.

Unicode slugs: preserve original characters in URLs. Browsers display them readably (現代日本/?lang=ja), but copy-paste produces percent-encoded URLs (%E7%8F%BE%E4%BB%A3...). Some platforms restrict Unicode slugs; check before relying on them.

When to choose Unicode: Japanese, Chinese, or Korean blog targeting native readers. The IDN URL appears clean to native users and Google indexes them well. The downside is sharing on platforms that show raw URLs.

sections.heading: Slug length and SEO
paragraphs: Google's URL display caps around 60-80 characters. Longer slugs get truncated and look 'spammy'. We default the max-length slider to 60 — short enough for most listings.

However, Google's algorithms don't penalize slug length directly. Length matters for click-through rates (humans skip long URLs) and for keeping the slug readable. Keywords in the slug help slightly with ranking; stuffing keywords into long slugs hurts more than helps.
Best practice: include the most important 2-3 keywords, drop stop words and articles, keep under 60 characters.

howTo.name: Type or paste a title
howTo.text: Article title, product name, or any descriptive text.

howTo.name: Pick a separator
howTo.text: Hyphen is standard. Underscore works for some legacy CMSs. Dots are unusual but valid.

howTo.name: Configure options
howTo.text: Lowercase (recommended), Unicode (for non-Latin URLs), max length cap.

howTo.name: Copy the slug
howTo.text: Paste into your CMS, blog post, or routing config.

faq.q: Should I use hyphens or underscores?
faq.a: Hyphens. Google explicitly treats hyphens as word separators in URLs; underscores are treated as part of the word. So 'fast-cars' is two keywords, 'fast_cars' is one. Hyphens win for SEO.

faq.q: Should I include stop words like 'the' or 'a'?
faq.a: Optional. Skipping them keeps slugs short. Including them keeps slugs readable. The SEO impact is negligible either way; readability matters more.
We strip them. 'don't' becomes 'dont'. Some generators preserve them as parts of words; we follow the simpler convention.
Yes — toggle 'Allow Unicode' to keep CJK characters in the slug. Without that flag, CJK characters are stripped (since they have no ASCII equivalent).

faq.q: Why is my Korean slug becoming empty in ASCII mode?
faq.a: Korean characters don't have direct ASCII equivalents. Either enable Unicode mode, or transliterate manually before pasting.
Yes in Unicode mode. URL-encoded emoji are valid but ugly — most platforms strip them. Practical advice: don't use emoji in slugs.

faq.q: What's the maximum slug length I should use?
faq.a: 60 characters or less is the sweet spot for SEO and CTR. Some CMS systems cap at 200 characters. Google's index supports much longer but rarely benefits readability.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Generation runs locally; nothing is sent to a server.
